name: decay_curve_exactly_half
description: time_ratio = 0.75 chosen so decay_curve = 0.5 EXACTLY under the M3 re-derivation: 1 - sqrt(1 - 0.75) = 1 - sqrt(0.25) = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 (rational sqrt; no IEEE-754 drift). At decay=0.5, dynamic_multiplier sits at MULT_OPEN - 0.5*(MULT_OPEN-MULT_CLOSE) = 1.0 EXACTLY (the midpoint of the linear-interp range), and dynamic_min_stop sits at 0.225 (with a single ulp of subtractive drift to 0.22499999999999998, absorbed by the rel-tolerance -- same 1-ulp pattern the pre-M3 fixture documented for log10's irrational midpoint). This 'algebraic clean point' pins the exact midpoint of the multiplier range under the new curve; a wrong impl that miscomputed the linear-interp coefficient would not produce 1.0 at this time_ratio. NOTE: under the OLD log10(1+9t) heuristic, decay=0.5 occurred at t ~= 0.2403; under the NEW 1-sqrt(1-t) curve, decay=0.5 occurs at t = 0.75 -- the algebraic-clean t-value MOVED because the curve shape moved. A wrong impl that left the old log10 heuristic in place would return dynamic_multiplier ~= 0.3266 here (not 1.0), making this fixture an excellent unchanged-formula detector.
provenance: DERIVED from the closed-form THEORY formula f(t) = 1 - sqrt(1 - t) (Danielsson & Zigrand, 2003, LSE FMG DP-439). NOT captured from compute_time_squeeze_decay output -- D-2 load-bearing per docs/research/m3-provenance/literature-pass.md §3.1.
derivation: time_ratio = 0.75 was chosen so that 1 - time_ratio = 0.25 (a perfect square), hence sqrt(0.25) = 0.5 EXACTLY, hence decay_curve = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 EXACTLY (no IEEE-754 sqrt drift, unlike the irrational cases). dynamic_multiplier = 1.5 - (1.5 - 0.5) * 0.5 = 1.5 - 0.5 = 1.0 EXACTLY. dynamic_min_stop = 0.3 - (0.3 - 0.15) * 0.5 = 0.3 - 0.075 = 0.225 (analytically exact; floating-point evaluation returns 0.22499999999999998 as a single-ulp negative drift from the final subtraction -- the rel-tolerance absorbs this, same ulp class as the pre-M3 fixture documented).
